club.name: Daylight Computer Club
club.tagline: Homebrew apps for your Daylight, shared between friends.

apps.name: Daylight Keys
apps.tagline: Volume keys become brightness keys, a snip screenshot tool, and the dog-ear corner.
apps.author: Anjan
apps.description: Press Vol Up + Vol Down together to toggle the volume keys between controlling volume and screen brightness — with fine single-unit steps at the dim end for nighttime reading, and a tap-the-sun backlight-off. Long-press the quick action button for a drag-a-rectangle screenshot snip. A folded dog-ear corner in the top-left reveals the time, your pomodoro, and your next event as you pull it.
inspected: permissions read straight from the app file
built from open source you can read
afterInstall: Open the Daylight Keys app from your home screen.
Turn on its accessibility service: open Settings, then Accessibility, find “dc1-keys”, and switch it on. (This is what lets it listen to the volume keys.)
Allow “Modify system settings” if the app asks — that's how it changes brightness.
Say yes to the notification, microphone, and calendar prompts if you want the dog-ear's timer and next-event features.

apps.name: Tracing Paper
apps.tagline: Press the top button — a sheet of glass slides over whatever you're reading. Jot the thought, press again, never lose your place.
apps.author: Anjan
apps.description: The club's own take on the transparent notepad — a from-scratch, readable-source homage to Matt Thompson's Glassnote (if you can, go support the real one). The DC-1's orange top button lays glass over any app; write on it with the pen (pressure and the eraser end both work — and the ink is low-latency: unbuffered stylus input drawn straight to a hardware surface with a velocity-predicted tail, so it keeps up with your hand). Keep as many notebooks as you like, each named, each on its own paper: blank, lined, dots, or school-ruled with a margin — and pages scroll past in one motion, discrete sheets on a continuous roll. HILITE is a light-gray marker ringed in thin black so highlights stand out when you scan a page. SNIP cuts a box out of whatever's on screen and pastes it on the glass right where it was, already selected: drag it around, resize it by a corner, tilt it — and it snaps straight when you get close to square. The GLASS slider takes the pad from clear to opaque paper, 0–100%. PEEK lets you scroll the page underneath while your ink stays put; SNAP (or holding the top button) saves a picture of notes-over-page; PDF exports every notebook as crisp vector ink to Download. The volume keys are deliberately untouched — Daylight Keys owns those. If a SolOS update ever remaps the top button, the app re-learns it with one press. Notes never leave the tablet.
permissions: watch the hardware buttons and draw the pad over other apps (its accessibility service)
take a screen picture — only when you press SNAP or SNIP, or hold the top button
inspected: permissions read straight from the app file
built from open source you can read
afterInstall: Open Tracing Paper from your home screen.
Turn on its pad service: open Settings, then Accessibility, find “Tracing Paper — glass pad”, and switch it on. (This is what lets the top button open the pad.)
Press the orange top button — glass slides over your page. Write with the pen; press the button again to put the glass away.
Hold the top button to save a picture of your notes over the page (it lands in Pictures).

On the glass, tap the ▾ button to make notebooks — pick blank, lined, dots, or school paper and give each a name. The GLASS slider makes the pad more or less see-through, and SNIP pastes a clipping of the page onto your glass.

apps.name: The Club itself
apps.tagline: Put this app-sharing hub on your home screen so it's always one tap away.
apps.author: Anjan
apps.description: The Daylight Computer Club is itself a web app. Add it to your home screen and it opens full-screen like a native app — new apps from friends show up here as they're shared.

apps.name: Club Companion
apps.tagline: Watches the shelf for you: new dishes, updates, recalls, one-tap installs — and it spots when apps fight.
apps.author: Anjan
apps.description: The club's own dish. A few times a day it looks at the shelf: a friend brought a dish → a quiet note; a dish got better → an update note; a dish was pulled → a recall note with what to do. Installing becomes one tap — the Companion fetches the dish and hands it to Android's installer (Android still asks you to confirm; nothing ever installs silently). Its inspector-at-home explains in plain words when two apps might fight over your keys or draw over each other, with a button to the exact Settings page. And if a dish misbehaves, one tap builds a report you can text to whoever made it — written so their Claude can read it and fix the dish. Everything it sees stays on your tablet.
permissions: ask Android to install apps (you confirm each one)
see what apps are installed (to spot collisions — never leaves the tablet)
inspected: permissions read straight from the app file
built from open source you can read
afterInstall: Open Club Companion from your home screen.
Allow notifications when it asks — that's how it tells you a friend brought a dish.
The first time you use a one-tap install, Android asks once to let Club Companion install apps: flip the switch and come back. Every install after that is one tap plus Android's confirm.
